title: practical english usage - Part 124
content:
grammar •  146   all: introduction She's invited all of you.  Emily sent all of them her love.

All of us can come tomorrow, (     All we...) All of us/you/them can be a subject or object.

With personal pronouns, we  use all of + us!you!them.

2 all   of   + personal       pronoun not All children can be difficult, (     All of children ...) Before a noun with no determiner, we  do not normally use of.

She's eaten all (of) the cake. All (of) my friends like riding.

both possible. All is more common than all of in British English.

Before a noun with a determiner (for example the, my, this), all and all of are All (of) can modify noun phrases and pronouns, especially in American English.

1  all   and   all   of 147 all   (of)  with    noun    phrases      andpronouns All, both and half follow similar grammar rules. For both, ►     155; for half, ►    478.

It's all because of you.  I looked all round, but I couldn't see anything.

I was all alone.     Tell me all about your plans.

You're all wet.       She walked all round the town.

All can be used to emphasise some adjectives, prepositions and adverbs.

5 all   with    adjectives,       adverbs,      etc:  all   alone All that matters is to be happy. That's all.

certain structures (►   149).

All can sometimes be used without a noun to mean 'everything' but only in 4 all   without      a  noun For more examples, ►     147-148.

All the people were singing. The people were all singing.

When  all modifies the subject, it can go either with the subject or with the verb.

3 with    the   subject      or  the   verb For details of word order, and the use of all of, ►     147.

Give my love to them all.

All (of) the people were singing. I haven't read all of it.

All modifies noun phrases or pronouns.

2  all   (of)  with    noun    phrases      and   pronouns not I'll take both shirts, (     ... aU two skirts.) I'll take all three shirts, please.

AU refers to three or more items. Compare: 1  three     or  more    items 146 all:  introduction grammar «148     all with the verb:  We can all swim.

The guests have all arrived. My family all work in education.

We can all swim.       Those apples were all bad.

in ’ mid-position’  (for details of word order, ►    200).

When all refers to the subject of a clause, it can go with the verb, 148 all   with    the   verb:   l/l/e  can    all   swim.

Not all birds can fly. No birds can play chess.

Note the difference between not all and no. Compare: Not all Americans like hamburgers.

affirmative verb.

(e.g. All Americans don't like hamburgers). We   more often use not all + noun + It is not very common to use all + noun as the subject of a negative verb 6 not    all   ...

She stayed here all day. (     ... allthe-day).

and all summer.

And we  usually leave out the in all day, all night, all week, all year, all winter all (the) three brothers After all, we  sometimes leave out the before numbers.

5 leaving      out   the (e.g. the whole story). For details, ►     150.

With other singular countable nouns, it is more natural to use whole I've read all (of) Shakespeare.

All (of) London knew about her affairs.

writers), especially in BrE.

We   can also use all (of) before proper nouns (e.g. the names of places or all that week all my family all the way things that can naturally be divided into parts.

However, all can be used before some singular countable nouns referring to all the water all my friends All is used mostly before uncountable and plural noun phrases.

4 types     of  noun For the American plural pronoun you all, ►     174.8.

(e.g. They have all gone home).

it is positioned with the verb (►   148) and may be separated from the pronoun All can follow a subject pronoun (e.g. They all went home), but in this case not    s 'Who did she invite?' 'All of us. (     U  all.)' not I think that's all of them, (     / thinkthat's-them-aU.) answers.

This does not generally happen with complement pronouns (after be) or in short I've made us all something to eat.

She's invited you all. Emily sent her love to them all.
